qTextArr: ['Poor Miss Taylor!--I wish she were here again. What a pity it is that\nMr. Weston ever thought of her!']
qText: Poor Miss Taylor!--I wish she were here again. What a pity it is that Mr. Weston ever thought of her!
speaker: Mr. Woodhouse

qTextArr:
['I cannot agree with you, papa; you know I cannot. Mr. Weston is such
a good-humoured, pleasant, excellent man, that he thoroughly deserves
a good wife;--and you would not have had Miss Taylor live with us for
ever, and bear all my odd humours, when she might have a house of her
own?']
qText: I cannot agree with you, papa; you know I cannot. Mr. Weston is such a good-humoured, pleasant, excellent man, that he thoroughly deserves a good wife;--and you would not have had Miss Taylor live with us for ever, and bear all my odd humours, when she might have a house of her own?
speaker: Emma
menEnts: [[['Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Mr. Weston'], ['Mr. Weston'], ['Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Mrs. Weston'], ['Emma', 'Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Mrs. Weston'], ['Mrs. Weston']]]

qTextArr: ['A house of her own!--But where is the advantage of a house of her own?\nThis is three times as large.--And you have never any odd humours, my\ndear.']
qText: A house of her own!--But where is the advantage of a house of her own? This is three times as large.--And you have never any odd humours, my dear.
speaker: Mr. Woodhouse

qTextArr: ['How often we shall be going to see them, and they coming to see us!--We\nshall be always meeting! _We_ must begin; we must go and pay wedding\nvisit very soon.']
qText: How often we shall be going to see them, and they coming to see us!--We shall be always meeting! _We_ must begin; we must go and pay wedding visit very soon.
speaker: Emma
menEnts: [[['Emma', 'Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Mr. Weston', 'Mrs. Weston'], ['Mr. Weston', 'Mrs. Weston'], ['Emma', 'Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Emma', 'Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Emma', 'Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Emma', 'Mr. Woodhouse']]]

qTextArr: ['My dear, how am I to get so far? Randalls is such a distance. I could\nnot walk half so far.']
qText: My dear, how am I to get so far? Randalls is such a distance. I could not walk half so far.
speaker: Mr. Woodhouse

qTextArr: ['No, papa, nobody thought of your walking. We must go in the carriage,\nto be sure.']
qText: No, papa, nobody thought of your walking. We must go in the carriage, to be sure.
speaker: Emma

qTextArr: ['The carriage! But James will not like to put the horses to for such a\nlittle way;--and where are the poor horses to be while we are paying our\nvisit?']
qText: The carriage! But James will not like to put the horses to for such a little way;--and where are the poor horses to be while we are paying our visit?
speaker: Mr. Woodhouse

qTextArr:
["They are to be put into Mr. Weston's stable, papa. You know we have
settled all that already. We talked it all over with Mr. Weston last
night. And as for James, you may be very sure he will always like going
to Randalls, because of his daughter's being housemaid there. I only
doubt whether he will ever take us anywhere else. That was your doing,
papa. You got Hannah that good place. Nobody thought of Hannah till you
mentioned her--James is so obliged to you!"]
qText: They are to be put into Mr. Weston's stable, papa. You know we have settled all that already. We talked it all over with Mr. Weston last night. And as for James, you may be very sure he will always like going to Randalls, because of his daughter's being housemaid there. I only doubt whether he will ever take us anywhere else. That was your doing, papa. You got Hannah that good place. Nobody thought of Hannah till you mentioned her--James is so obliged to you!
speaker: Emma
menEnts: [[['Mr. Weston'], ['Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Emma'], ['Emma'], ['Mr. Weston'], ['Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Emma', 'Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Mr. Woodhouse']]]

qTextArr:
['I am very glad I did think of her. It was very lucky, for I would not
have had poor James think himself slighted upon any account; and I am
sure she will make a very good servant: she is a civil, pretty-spoken
girl; I have a great opinion of her. Whenever I see her, she always
curtseys and asks me how I do, in a very pretty manner; and when you
have had her here to do needlework, I observe she always turns the lock
of the door the right way and never bangs it. I am sure she will be an
excellent servant; and it will be a great comfort to poor Miss Taylor
to have somebody about her that she is used to see. Whenever James goes
over to see his daughter, you know, she will be hearing of us. He will
be able to tell her how we all are.']
qText: I am very glad I did think of her. It was very lucky, for I would not have had poor James think himself slighted upon any account; and I am sure she will make a very good servant: she is a civil, pretty-spoken girl; I have a great opinion of her. Whenever I see her, she always curtseys and asks me how I do, in a very pretty manner; and when you have had her here to do needlework, I observe she always turns the lock of the door the right way and never bangs it. I am sure she will be an excellent servant; and it will be a great comfort to poor Miss Taylor to have somebody about her that she is used to see. Whenever James goes over to see his daughter, you know, she will be hearing of us. He will be able to tell her how we all are.
speaker: Mr. Woodhouse
menEnts: [[['Mrs. Weston'], ['Mrs. Weston'], ['Mrs. Weston'], ['Emma'], ['Mrs. Weston'], ['Emma', 'Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Mrs. Weston'], ['Emma', 'Mr. Woodhouse']]]

qTextArr: ['It is very kind of you, Mr. Knightley,\nto come out at this late hour to call upon us. I am afraid you must have\nhad a shocking walk.']
qText: It is very kind of you, Mr. Knightley, to come out at this late hour to call upon us. I am afraid you must have had a shocking walk.
speaker: Mr. Woodhouse

qTextArr: ['Not at all, sir. It is a beautiful moonlight night; and so mild that I\nmust draw back from your great fire.']
qText: Not at all, sir. It is a beautiful moonlight night; and so mild that I must draw back from your great fire.
speaker: Mr. Knightley

qTextArr: ['But you must have found it very damp and dirty. I wish you may not\ncatch cold.']
qText: But you must have found it very damp and dirty. I wish you may not catch cold.
speaker: Mr. Woodhouse

qTextArr: ['Dirty, sir! Look at my shoes. Not a speck on them.']
qText: Dirty, sir! Look at my shoes. Not a speck on them.
speaker: Mr. Knightley

qTextArr: ['Well! that is quite surprising, for we have had a vast deal of rain\nhere. It rained dreadfully hard for half an hour while we were at\nbreakfast. I wanted them to put off the wedding.']
qText: Well! that is quite surprising, for we have had a vast deal of rain here. It rained dreadfully hard for half an hour while we were at breakfast. I wanted them to put off the wedding.
speaker: Mr. Woodhouse

qTextArr:
['By the bye--I have not wished you joy. Being pretty well aware of what
sort of joy you must both be feeling, I have been in no hurry with my
congratulations; but I hope it all went off tolerably well. How did you
all behave? Who cried most?']
qText: By the bye--I have not wished you joy. Being pretty well aware of what sort of joy you must both be feeling, I have been in no hurry with my congratulations; but I hope it all went off tolerably well. How did you all behave? Who cried most?
speaker: Mr. Knightley

qTextArr: ["Ah! poor Miss Taylor! 'Tis a sad business."]
qText: Ah! poor Miss Taylor! 'Tis a sad business.
speaker: Mr. Woodhouse

qTextArr: ['Poor Mr. and Miss Woodhouse, if you please; but I cannot possibly say']
qText: Poor Mr. and Miss Woodhouse, if you please; but I cannot possibly say
speaker: Mr. Knightley

qTextArr: ['I have a great regard for you and Emma; but when it\ncomes to the question of dependence or independence!--At any rate, it\nmust be better to have only one to please than two.']
qText: I have a great regard for you and Emma; but when it comes to the question of dependence or independence!--At any rate, it must be better to have only one to please than two.
speaker: Mr. Knightley

qTextArr: ['Especially when _one_ of those two is such a fanciful, troublesome\ncreature!', 'That is what you have in your head, I\nknow--and what you would certainly say if my father were not by.']
qText: Especially when _one_ of those two is such a fanciful, troublesome creature! That is what you have in your head, I know--and what you would certainly say if my father were not by.
speaker: Emma

qTextArr: ['I believe it is very true, my dear, indeed,', 'I am afraid I am sometimes very fanciful and troublesome.']
qText: I believe it is very true, my dear, indeed, I am afraid I am sometimes very fanciful and troublesome.
speaker: Mr. Woodhouse

qTextArr:
['My dearest papa! You do not think I could mean _you_, or suppose Mr.
Knightley to mean _you_. What a horrible idea! Oh no! I meant only
myself. Mr. Knightley loves to find fault with me, you know--in a
joke--it is all a joke. We always say what we like to one another.']
qText: My dearest papa! You do not think I could mean _you_, or suppose Mr. Knightley to mean _you_. What a horrible idea! Oh no! I meant only myself. Mr. Knightley loves to find fault with me, you know--in a joke--it is all a joke. We always say what we like to one another.
speaker: Emma
menEnts: [[['Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Mr. Knightley'], ['Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Mr. Knightley'], ['Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Emma', 'Mr. Knightley'], ['Emma', 'Mr. Knightley']]]

qTextArr:
['Emma knows I never flatter her,', 'but I meant no
reflection on any body. Miss Taylor has been used to have two persons
to please; she will now have but one. The chances are that she must be a
gainer.']
qText: Emma knows I never flatter her, but I meant no reflection on any body. Miss Taylor has been used to have two persons to please; she will now have but one. The chances are that she must be a gainer.
speaker: Mr. Knightley

qTextArr:
['Well,', 'you want to hear about
the wedding; and I shall be happy to tell you, for we all behaved
charmingly. Every body was punctual, every body in their best looks: not
a tear, and hardly a long face to be seen. Oh no; we all felt that we
were going to be only half a mile apart, and were sure of meeting every
day.']
qText: Well, you want to hear about the wedding; and I shall be happy to tell you, for we all behaved charmingly. Every body was punctual, every body in their best looks: not a tear, and hardly a long face to be seen. Oh no; we all felt that we were going to be only half a mile apart, and were sure of meeting every day.
speaker: Emma
menEnts: [[], [['Mr. Knightley'], ['Mr. Knightley'], ['Emma', 'Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Emma', 'Mr. Woodhouse', 'Mr. Weston', 'Mrs. Weston'], ['Emma', 'Mr. Woodhouse', 'Mr. Weston', 'Mrs. Weston']]]

qTextArr: ['Dear Emma bears every thing so well,', 'But, Mr.\nKnightley, she is really very sorry to lose poor Miss Taylor, and I am\nsure she _will_ miss her more than she thinks for.']
qText: Dear Emma bears every thing so well, But, Mr. Knightley, she is really very sorry to lose poor Miss Taylor, and I am sure she _will_ miss her more than she thinks for.
speaker: Mr. Woodhouse

qTextArr:
['It
is impossible that Emma should not miss such a companion,', "We should not like her so well as we do, sir, if we could
suppose it; but she knows how much the marriage is to Miss Taylor's
advantage; she knows how very acceptable it must be, at Miss Taylor's
time of life, to be settled in a home of her own, and how important to
her to be secure of a comfortable provision, and therefore cannot allow
herself to feel so much pain as pleasure. Every friend of Miss Taylor
must be glad to have her so happily married."]
qText: It is impossible that Emma should not miss such a companion, We should not like her so well as we do, sir, if we could suppose it; but she knows how much the marriage is to Miss Taylor's advantage; she knows how very acceptable it must be, at Miss Taylor's time of life, to be settled in a home of her own, and how important to her to be secure of a comfortable provision, and therefore cannot allow herself to feel so much pain as pleasure. Every friend of Miss Taylor must be glad to have her so happily married.
speaker: Mr. Knightley
menEnts: [[['Emma']], [['Knightley', 'Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Knightley', 'Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Knightley', 'Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Emma'], ['Mrs. Weston'], ['Emma'], ['Mrs. Weston'], ['Mrs. Weston'], ['Mrs. Weston'], ['Mrs. Weston'], ['Mrs. Weston']]]

qTextArr:
['And you have forgotten one matter of joy to me,', 'and a very
considerable one--that I made the match myself. I made the match, you
know, four years ago; and to have it take place, and be proved in the
right, when so many people said Mr. Weston would never marry again, may
comfort me for any thing.']
qText: And you have forgotten one matter of joy to me, and a very considerable one--that I made the match myself. I made the match, you know, four years ago; and to have it take place, and be proved in the right, when so many people said Mr. Weston would never marry again, may comfort me for any thing.
speaker: Emma

qTextArr:
['Ah!
my dear, I wish you would not make matches and foretell things, for
whatever you say always comes to pass. Pray do not make any more
matches.']
qText: Ah! my dear, I wish you would not make matches and foretell things, for whatever you say always comes to pass. Pray do not make any more matches.
speaker: Mr. Woodhouse

qTextArr:
['I promise you to make none for myself, papa; but I must, indeed, for
other people. It is the greatest amusement in the world! And after such
success, you know!--Every body said that Mr. Weston would never marry
again. Oh dear, no! Mr. Weston, who had been a widower so long, and who
seemed so perfectly comfortable without a wife, so constantly occupied
either in his business in town or among his friends here, always
acceptable wherever he went, always cheerful--Mr. Weston need not spend
a single evening in the year alone if he did not like it. Oh no! Mr.
Weston certainly would never marry again. Some people even talked of a
promise to his wife on her deathbed, and others of the son and the
uncle not letting him. All manner of solemn nonsense was talked on the
subject, but I believed none of it.']
qText: I promise you to make none for myself, papa; but I must, indeed, for other people. It is the greatest amusement in the world! And after such success, you know!--Every body said that Mr. Weston would never marry again. Oh dear, no! Mr. Weston, who had been a widower so long, and who seemed so perfectly comfortable without a wife, so constantly occupied either in his business in town or among his friends here, always acceptable wherever he went, always cheerful--Mr. Weston need not spend a single evening in the year alone if he did not like it. Oh no! Mr. Weston certainly would never marry again. Some people even talked of a promise to his wife on her deathbed, and others of the son and the uncle not letting him. All manner of solemn nonsense was talked on the subject, but I believed none of it.
speaker: Emma
menEnts: [[['Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Mr. Weston'], ['Mr. Weston'], ['Mr. Weston'], ['Mr. Weston'], ['Mr. Weston'], ['Mr. Weston'], ['Mr. Weston'], ['Mr. Weston'], ['Mr. Weston'], ['Frank Churchill'], ['Mr. Weston']]]

qTextArr:
["Ever since the day--about four years ago--that Miss Taylor and I met
with him in Broadway Lane, when, because it began to drizzle, he darted
away with so much gallantry, and borrowed two umbrellas for us from
Farmer Mitchell's, I made up my mind on the subject. I planned the match
from that hour; and when such success has blessed me in this instance,
dear papa, you cannot think that I shall leave off match-making."]
qText: Ever since the day--about four years ago--that Miss Taylor and I met with him in Broadway Lane, when, because it began to drizzle, he darted away with so much gallantry, and borrowed two umbrellas for us from Farmer Mitchell's, I made up my mind on the subject. I planned the match from that hour; and when such success has blessed me in this instance, dear papa, you cannot think that I shall leave off match-making.
speaker: Emma
menEnts: [[['Mrs. Weston'], ['Mr. Weston'], ['Mr. Weston'], ['Emma', 'Mrs. Weston'], ['Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Mr. Woodhouse']]]

qTextArr:
["I do not understand what you mean by 'success,'", "Success supposes endeavour. Your time has been properly and delicately
spent, if you have been endeavouring for the last four years to bring
about this marriage. A worthy employment for a young lady's mind! But
if, which I rather imagine, your making the match, as you call it, means
only your planning it, your saying to yourself one idle day, 'I think it
would be a very good thing for Miss Taylor if Mr. Weston were to marry
her,' and saying it again to yourself every now and then afterwards, why
do you talk of success? Where is your merit? What are you proud of? You
made a lucky guess; and _that_ is all that can be said."]
qText: I do not understand what you mean by 'success,' Success supposes endeavour. Your time has been properly and delicately spent, if you have been endeavouring for the last four years to bring about this marriage. A worthy employment for a young lady's mind! But if, which I rather imagine, your making the match, as you call it, means only your planning it, your saying to yourself one idle day, 'I think it would be a very good thing for Miss Taylor if Mr. Weston were to marry her,' and saying it again to yourself every now and then afterwards, why do you talk of success? Where is your merit? What are you proud of? You made a lucky guess; and _that_ is all that can be said.
speaker: Mr. Knightley

qTextArr:
["And have you never known the pleasure and triumph of a lucky guess?--I
pity you.--I thought you cleverer--for, depend upon it a lucky guess is
never merely luck. There is always some talent in it. And as to my
poor word 'success,' which you quarrel with, I do not know that I am so
entirely without any claim to it. You have drawn two pretty pictures;
but I think there may be a third--a something between the do-nothing and
the do-all. If I had not promoted Mr. Weston's visits here, and given
many little encouragements, and smoothed many little matters, it might
not have come to any thing after all. I think you must know Hartfield
enough to comprehend that."]
qText: And have you never known the pleasure and triumph of a lucky guess?--I pity you.--I thought you cleverer--for, depend upon it a lucky guess is never merely luck. There is always some talent in it. And as to my poor word 'success,' which you quarrel with, I do not know that I am so entirely without any claim to it. You have drawn two pretty pictures; but I think there may be a third--a something between the do-nothing and the do-all. If I had not promoted Mr. Weston's visits here, and given many little encouragements, and smoothed many little matters, it might not have come to any thing after all. I think you must know Hartfield enough to comprehend that.
speaker: Emma

qTextArr:
['A straightforward, open-hearted man like Weston, and a rational,
unaffected woman like Miss Taylor, may be safely left to manage their
own concerns. You are more likely to have done harm to yourself, than
good to them, by interference.']
qText: A straightforward, open-hearted man like Weston, and a rational, unaffected woman like Miss Taylor, may be safely left to manage their own concerns. You are more likely to have done harm to yourself, than good to them, by interference.
speaker: Mr. Knightley
menEnts: [[['Mr. Weston'], ['Mrs. Weston'], ['Mrs. Weston', 'Mr. Weston'], ['Mr. Weston', 'Mrs. Weston']]]

qTextArr: ['Emma never thinks of herself, if she can do good to others,', "But, my dear, pray do not\nmake any more matches; they are silly things, and break up one's family\ncircle grievously."]
qText: Emma never thinks of herself, if she can do good to others, But, my dear, pray do not make any more matches; they are silly things, and break up one's family circle grievously.
speaker: Mr. Woodhouse

qTextArr:
['Only one more, papa; only for Mr. Elton. Poor Mr. Elton! You like Mr.
Elton, papa,--I must look about for a wife for him. There is nobody in
Highbury who deserves him--and he has been here a whole year, and has
fitted up his house so comfortably, that it would be a shame to have him
single any longer--and I thought when he was joining their hands to-day,
he looked so very much as if he would like to have the same kind office
done for him! I think very well of Mr. Elton, and this is the only way I
have of doing him a service.']
qText: Only one more, papa; only for Mr. Elton. Poor Mr. Elton! You like Mr. Elton, papa,--I must look about for a wife for him. There is nobody in Highbury who deserves him--and he has been here a whole year, and has fitted up his house so comfortably, that it would be a shame to have him single any longer--and I thought when he was joining their hands to-day, he looked so very much as if he would like to have the same kind office done for him! I think very well of Mr. Elton, and this is the only way I have of doing him a service.
speaker: Emma

qTextArr:
['Mr. Elton is a very pretty young man, to be sure, and a very good young
man, and I have a great regard for him. But if you want to shew him any
attention, my dear, ask him to come and dine with us some day. That will
be a much better thing. I dare say Mr. Knightley will be so kind as to
meet him.']
qText: Mr. Elton is a very pretty young man, to be sure, and a very good young man, and I have a great regard for him. But if you want to shew him any attention, my dear, ask him to come and dine with us some day. That will be a much better thing. I dare say Mr. Knightley will be so kind as to meet him.
speaker: Mr. Woodhouse
menEnts: [[['Mr. Elton'], ['Mr. Elton'], ['Emma'], ['Mr. Elton'], ['Emma'], ['Mr. Elton'], ['Emma', 'Mr. Woodhouse'], ['Mr. Knightley'], ['Mr. Elton']]]

qTextArr:
['With a great deal of pleasure, sir, at any time,', 'and I agree with you entirely, that it will be a much better
thing. Invite him to dinner, Emma, and help him to the best of the fish
and the chicken, but leave him to chuse his own wife. Depend upon it, a
man of six or seven-and-twenty can take care of himself.']
qText: With a great deal of pleasure, sir, at any time, and I agree with you entirely, that it will be a much betterthing. Invite him to dinner, Emma, and help him to the best of the fish and the chicken, but leave him to chuse his own wife. Depend upon it, a man of six or seven-and-twenty can take care of himself.
speaker: Mr. Knightley
